title: Homosexual Acts (Liwat)
subtitle: The Sin of the People of Lut, Condemned in the Quran

verses.arabic: وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ الْعَالَمِينَ
verses.translation: And [We had sent] Lot, when he said to his people, 'Do you commit such immorality as no one has preceded you with from among the worlds?'

verses.arabic: إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِّن دُونِ النِّسَاءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ
verses.translation: Indeed, you approach men with desire, instead of women. Rather, you are a transgressing people.
Do you approach males among the worlds

brief_explanation: The Quran recounts the story of the people of Lut across multiple surahs, presenting their act of homosexual intercourse as a uniquely unprecedented fahishah — an immorality without precedent in all of creation. Allah destroyed the people of Lut with a catastrophic punishment of stones rained from the sky, their city overturned — a worldly punishment recorded in the Quran as a permanent sign and warning for all generations. The Quran describes them as 'musrifun' (transgressors who cross all limits), indicating that this act is a complete departure from the fitrah (innate nature) Allah created humanity upon. Their story is referenced in no fewer than seven surahs, each time accompanied by divine wrath and destruction.
capital_punishment: All four major madhabs agree that liwat (anal intercourse between males) is a hadd offense. They differ on the punishment: the Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali schools hold that the punishment is the same as zina — 100 lashes for the unmarried and death for the married — or death in all cases according to one Hanbali and Shafi'i position. The Hanafi school holds that there is no prescribed hadd for liwat specifically, but that severe ta'zir (discretionary punishment up to death) is applied. The act is unanimously considered a major crime in Islamic law.
